response_message.content:
Below is a quick‑reference guide to the most‑used open‑source evaluation frameworks for LLMs (and a few related tooling pieces), followed by the high‑level decision matrix you can use to pick one of them — or decide to roll your own.

---

## 1️⃣ Popular frameworks (what they give you out‑of‑the‑box)

| Framework | Primary language | Model‑provider support | Benchmark / task catalogue | Custom metric / prompt support | UI / reporting | Licensing / community | Typical use‑case |
|-----------|------------------|------------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|----------------|-----------------------|------------------|
| **OpenAI Evals** | Python | OpenAI (ChatGPT, GPT‑4, embeddings) – can be wrapped around any HTTP‑compatible API via the `OpenAI` client | Built‑in registry of ~200 evals (e.g. `grade_multiple_choice`, `function_calling`, `hallucination`) | Write a Python class that inherits from `Eval` – you can plug in arbitrary prompts, few‑shot examples, custom scoring functions | Dashboard integration (run from the OpenAI platform) + CLI output | Apache‑2.0, active GitHub community (≈ 18 k ★) | You already use OpenAI models and want a low‑friction way to add private or public evals without managing data pipelines. |
| **LM‑Evaluation‑Harness** (EleutherAI) | Python | Any HF‑compatible model, VLLM, SGLang, custom `local-completions` wrapper – works with open‑source LLMs, hosted APIs, and even multimodal back‑ends | 400+ tasks (MMLU, BIG‑Bench, GPQA, etc.) + YAML‑based task definition; supports Jinja2 prompt templating & Promptsource integration | Task YAML lets you specify prompt, few‑shot, answer extraction, post‑processing; you can add new tasks by dropping a YAML file or a Python `Task` subclass | CLI table & JSON output; can pipe to external dashboards; community scripts for W&B, TensorBoard | MIT, very active community (≈ 12 k ★) | You need a flexible, model‑agnostic benchmark suite for research or for evaluating many open‑source LLMs on standard NLP tasks. |
| **HELM** (Holistic Evaluation of Language Models) | Python | Unified adapters for OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Azure, local HF, vLLM, etc. | Curated “capabilities” and “risk” benchmark suites (MMLU‑Pro, GPQA, IFEval, WildBench, toxicity, bias, latency, compute) | Metrics are first‑class objects; you can register new metrics or combine existing ones; YAML suite files let you mix tasks, models, and evaluation configs | Full‑stack web UI (benchmark explorer, per‑prompt inspection) + leaderboard website; CLI for batch runs and summarisation | Apache‑2.0, Stanford‑CRFM backing (≈ 2.7 k ★) | You want a *holistic* view of a model—accuracy **and** efficiency, safety, bias—plus a reproducible leaderboard for papers or internal model‑selection. |
| **🤗 Evaluate** (HuggingFace) | Python | Pure metric library – works with any model framework (HF, TensorFlow, PyTorch, JAX, custom) | > 150 built‑in metrics (accuracy, BLEU, ROUGE, F1, image‑classification, captioning, etc.) | Add new metric by subclassing `Metric` and pushing a metric card to the Hub; can be used from the CLI (`evaluate load "accuracy"`) | Simple CLI + Markdown cards on the Hub; no dedicated UI for multi‑model runs | Apache‑2.0, very active (≈ 2.4 k ★) | You only need metric computation (e.g., precision/recall, BLEU) and want to reuse the same metric across many experiments. |
| **LangChain Eval** (experimental) | Python | Works with any LangChain‑compatible LLM (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, HuggingFace) | Provides `Evaluator` abstractions for question‑answering, retrieval‑augmented generation, tool‑calling, chain‑level scoring | You can write custom evaluators that compare LLM output to reference answers, compute semantic similarity, or run a downstream test suite | Integrated in LangChain notebooks and can be logged to LangSmith (observability platform) | MIT, part of the larger LangChain ecosystem | You already build LLM‑driven agents/chains with LangChain and want evaluation to be a first‑class step in the same pipeline. |
| **LightEval** (HuggingFace) – newer LLM‑specific library | Python | Focused on LLMs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, HF) | Growing set of LLM‑centric tasks (reasoning, code, safety) | Plug‑and‑play evaluation scripts, easy to add custom tasks via YAML | CLI + optional UI via LangSmith | Apache‑2.0, still maturing | You want a lightweight successor to 🤗 Evaluate that is more tuned for LLM‑style generation tasks. |

> **TL;DR:** If you need a **quick, OpenAI‑only** solution → **OpenAI Evals**.  
> If you need **model‑agnostic, many standard NLP tasks** → **LM‑Eval‑Harness**.  
> If you need a **holistic view (accuracy + efficiency + risk)** → **HELM**.  
> If you just need **metric computation** → **🤗 Evaluate** (or LightEval).  
> If you’re already inside the **LangChain** ecosystem → **LangChain Eval**.

---

## 2️⃣ Decision matrix – what to ask yourself first

| Question | What to look for in a framework | Recommended pick |
|----------|----------------------------------|------------------|
| **Which model providers do I use?** | Single provider (OpenAI) → minimal wrapper; multiple providers (HF, Anthropic, Gemini, local) → need a unified adapter. | OpenAI Evals (single); LM‑Eval‑Harness, HELM, LangChain Eval (multiple). |
| **Do I need a curated benchmark suite or will I write my own tasks?** | Want out‑of‑the‑box tasks → pick a framework with a large task catalogue. | LM‑Eval‑Harness (400+), HELM (capability + risk suites). |
| **Is reproducibility & leader‑board publishing important?** | Need versioned runs, public UI, shareable results. | HELM (web UI + leaderboard), OpenAI Evals (Dashboard). |
| **Do I need multi‑modal support (image, audio, video)?** | Look for back‑ends that expose `hf-multimodal`, `vllm‑vlm`, or custom pipelines. | HELM (experimental multimodal), LM‑Eval‑Harness (vLLM‑multimodal). |
| **How much engineering bandwidth do I have?** | Want “install‑and‑run” with minimal code → high‑level CLI. | OpenAI Evals, HELM (simple CLI). |
| **Do I already use a specific ecosystem (LangChain, HF Hub, W&B, etc.)?** | Prefer native integration. | LangChain Eval (LangSmith), 🤗 Evaluate (Hub cards), HELM (W&B scripts). |
| **Do I need custom metrics that are not just accuracy/bleu?** | Ability to write arbitrary Python metric functions, combine multiple signals. | HELM (efficiency, toxicity, bias), LM‑Eval‑Harness (post‑processing hooks), 🤗 Evaluate (custom metric subclass). |
| **Is data privacy a concern?** | Must keep prompts / model responses on‑prem. | All open‑source frameworks (LM‑Eval‑Harness, HELM, 🤗 Evaluate) can run entirely locally; OpenAI Evals can be used privately by storing data in your own repo and disabling telemetry. |
| **Budget / cost** | Evaluate large models locally vs. pay‑per‑token API. | If you have GPU resources → LM‑Eval‑Harness + vLLM. If you rely on API → OpenAI Evals (cost tracked via API usage). |
| **Future extensibility** | Will you need to add new model adapters, prompt‑templating languages, or integrate CI pipelines? | LM‑Eval‑Harness (YAML + Python plug‑ins), HELM (modular adapters), LangChain Eval (chain‑level hooks). |

---

## 3️⃣ When to build your own

Even with the frameworks above, you might still need a custom solution. Consider writing your own pipeline if **any** of the following hold:

1. **Very niche domain** (e.g., legal contract generation with custom compliance checks) where existing benchmarks do not reflect the downstream success criteria.
2. **Tight integration with internal tooling** (e.g., a proprietary prompt‑store, experiment tracker, or security scanner) that cannot be cleanly hooked into the public APIs of the existing libraries.
3. **Performance‑critical, massive‑scale evaluation** (billions of prompts) where you need a custom distributed runner (e.g., Spark, Ray) that the standard CLI cannot drive.
4. **Non‑Python stack** – if your production codebase lives in Rust, Go, or Node.js and you cannot afford the overhead of a Python subprocess, a thin wrapper around the model API plus a hand‑rolled metrics collector may be simpler.
5. **Policy or compliance restrictions** that forbid any external dependencies (including open‑source libraries with unclear licenses).  

If you decide to go down the DIY route, a good skeleton looks like:

```python
# pseudo‑code – keep it minimal and reusable
class EvalRun:
    def __init__(self, model, prompts, scorer):
        self.model = model          # any callable that returns a string
        self.prompts = prompts      # list[dict] or generator
        self.scorer = scorer        # Callable[(prompt, response) -> dict]

    def execute(self):
        results = []
        for p in self.prompts:
            resp = self.model(p["input"])
            score = self.scorer(p, resp)
            results.append({**p, "response": resp, **score})
        return results

# Example usage
def openai_chat(prompt):
    # thin wrapper around openai.ChatCompletion.create …
    return ...

def exact_match_scorer(prompt, response):
    return {"accuracy": int(response.strip() == prompt["expected"])}
```

From there you can plug in:

* **Parallelism** (`concurrent.futures`, Ray, multiprocessing)  
* **Logging / tracking** (W&B, MLflow, CSV)  
* **Reporting** (HTML table, Plotly dashboards)  

---

## 4️⃣ Suggested next steps for you

1. **List your constraints** – model providers, data‑privacy, required metrics, expected scale.  
2. **Prototype the simplest fit** – e.g., `pip install openai-evals && openai-evals run grade_multiple_choice` if you’re already on OpenAI.  
3. **Run a small cross‑validation** – pick 2‑3 representative prompts, evaluate with both OpenAI Evals and LM‑Eval‑Harness (the latter can be pointed at the same OpenAI API via the `openai` backend). Compare ease of adding a custom metric.  
4. **Scale up** – if you need multi‑modal or holistic metrics, switch to HELM or LM‑Eval‑Harness with VLLM for GPU‑accelerated inference.  
5. **Integrate with your CI** – all three major frameworks provide a CLI that returns JSON; add a step that asserts a minimum accuracy or latency threshold before merging.  

---

### TL;DR

| Goal | Best‑fit framework |
|------|-------------------|
| **OpenAI‑only, quick private evals** | **OpenAI Evals** |
| **Open‑source LLMs, many standard NLP tasks** | **LM‑Eval‑Harness** |
| **Holistic (accuracy + efficiency + risk) & public leaderboard** | **HELM** |
| **Just metric computation (BLEU, ROUGE, F1…)** | **🤗 Evaluate** (or LightEval) |
| **Already in LangChain, want chain‑level scores** | **LangChain Eval** |
| **Custom, domain‑specific, high‑scale pipeline** | **Build your own** (use the skeleton above) |

Feel free to ask for a concrete example (e.g., a minimal OpenAI Evals script, a LM‑Eval‑Harness YAML task, or a HELM suite file) and I can spin one up for you. Happy evaluating!

You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo.

## Ground rules when writing code:

- Follow the user's instructions carefully.
- Do not explain answers using code comments.
- NEVER change **unrelated code and comments.**
- Never add new comments to the end of a line.
- Never add trivial comments.
- Respect indentation when modifying code.
- Prefer readable code over of comments.
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Tool use

Never modify files outside of the current working directory unless requested.

Current directory: ~/repos/github/g0t4/ask-openai.nvim

## Use `semantic_grep` to find code

The semantic_grep tool:

- has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase
- enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval)
- includes re-ranker
- it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it!
- while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg`

## Committing

- `git diff` is a sanity check!
- COMMIT when you're done.
- Include "Co-Authored-By: gptoss120b"


## 🙌  TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS

- 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents
- 🙌 `fd` to search file names

## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS

If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom.

- ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f`

Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions.


## Use `apply_patch` to edit files.

Your patch language is a stripped‑down, file‑oriented diff format designed to be easy to parse and safe to apply. You can think of it as a high‑level envelope:

*** Begin Patch
[ one or more file sections ]
*** End Patch

Within that envelope, you get a sequence of file operations.
You MUST include a header to specify the action you are taking.
Each operation starts with one of three headers:

*** Add File: <path> - create a new file. Every following line is a + line (the initial contents).
*** Delete File: <path> - remove an existing file. Nothing follows.
*** Update File: <path> - patch an existing file in place (optionally with a rename).

If you want to rename the file too, immediately follow with:
*** Move to: <new path>

Then one or more “hunks”, each introduced by @@ (optionally followed by a hunk header).
Within a hunk each line starts with:

- for inserted text,
* for removed text, or
  space ( ) for context.
  At the end of a truncated hunk you can emit *** End of File.

Here is the grammar:

Patch := Begin { FileOp } End
Begin := "*** Begin Patch" NEWLINE
End := "*** End Patch" NEWLINE
FileOp := AddFile | DeleteFile | UpdateFile
AddFile := "*** Add File: " path NEWLINE { "+" line NEWLINE }
DeleteFile := "*** Delete File: " path NEWLINE
UpdateFile := "*** Update File: " path NEWLINE [ MoveTo ] { Hunk }
MoveTo := "*** Move to: " newPath NEWLINE
Hunk := "@@" [ header ] NEWLINE { HunkLine } [ "*** End of File" NEWLINE ]
HunkLine := (" " | "-" | "+") text NEWLINE

A full patch can combine several operations:

*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: hello.txt
+Hello world
*** Update File: src/app.py
*** Move to: src/main.py
@@ def greet():
-print("Hi")
+print("Hello, world!")
*** Delete File: obsolete.txt
*** End Patch

It is important to remember:

- You must include a header with your intended action (Add/Delete/Update)
- You must prefix new lines with `+` even when creating a new file


Tips on a failure:

- You only need enough context for a unique match!
- The line(s) you replace are context too; if they’re unique, no additional context is needed.
- Split bigger patches into smaller patches, i.e. if editing two functions, try each separately!
- Watch out for whitespace differences, i.e. blank lines

Please be curteous:

- NEVER leave comments about removed code, just get rid of it. This code lives in a git repo.
- Check your work! Especially after a failure.

### More Examples

Here's a patch file to change the `win` property's type:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@ @dataclass
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
-    gap_rms: TimeSeries
-    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
+    gap_rms: TimeSeries
+    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
*** End Patch
```

Which can be simplified to:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

Or, this could work if the `win` property is unique:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-    win: GapWindow
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

## General Code Preferences

- When rewriting code, leave unrelated code and unrelated comments as is.
- When an if statement has an AMBIGUOUS condition, extract a variable to meaningfully name it, for example:
```lua
local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$")
if is_blank_line then
    -- ...
end
```

## Lua Code Preferences

Naming
- Use snake_case for functions and variables.
- Use PascalCase for classes.

Type Hints
- Prefer EmmyLua/LuaLS style annotations
- Always annotate when type inference would be ambiguous, or for public APIs.

Examples:
```lua
---@param names string[]
---@param checker fun(name: string): boolean
---@return table<string, integer>
function process_names(names, checker) end

---@class WindowController
---@field window hs.axuielement
---@field title? string
---@field size { w: number, h: number }
local WindowController = {}
```

This framework includes the following features:\n\n- Datasets and benchmarks in a standardized format (e.g. MMLU-Pro, GPQA, IFEval, WildBench)\n- Models from various providers accessible through a unified interface (e.g. OpenAI models, Anthropic Claude, Google Gemini)\n- Metrics for measuring various aspects beyond accuracy (e.g. efficiency, bias, toxicity)\n- Web UI for inspecting individual prompts and responses\n- Web leaderboard for comparing results across models and benchmarks\n\n## Documentation\n\nPlease refer to [the documentation on Read the Docs](https://crfm-helm.readthedocs.io/) for instructions on how to install and run HELM.\n\n## Quick Start\n\n\n\nInstall the package from PyPI:\n\n```sh\npip install crfm-helm\n```\n\nRun the following in your shell:\n\n```sh\n# Run benchmark\nhelm-run --run-entries mmlu:subject=philosophy,model=openai/gpt2 --suite my-suite --max-eval-instances 10\n\n# Summarize benchmark results\nhelm-summarize --suite my-suite\n\n# Start a web server to display benchmark results\nhelm-server --suite my-suite\n```\n\nThen go to http://localhost:8000/ in your browser.\n\n\n\n## Leaderboards\n\nWe maintain offical leaderboards with results from evaluating recent models on notable benchmarks using this framework.
